Case Study — 001

Fashion
Brand

E-commerce UX Design UI Design Design System Responsive
Role
UX & UI Designer
Type
Client Project
Deliverables
Full e-commerce design
Platform
Web — Desktop & Mobile
Overview
The Brief

A niche fashion brand approached me to design their e-commerce presence from the ground up. Their product is stripped-down, conceptual clothing — not trend-driven, not mass-market. The clothes are austere, sculptural, and rooted in raw honesty.

The goal was to create a minimalist digital experience that feels just as intentional as the product itself — translating the brand's philosophy into every screen, spacing decision, and interaction.

"Our vision is grounded in raw honesty and a refusal to chase trends. Each piece is stripped to its essential form — austere, sculptural, and unapologetically human."
— Brand manifesto
The Challenge

The biggest challenge wasn't technical — it was timing. The brand was developing its identity, tone, and product direction in parallel with the website. I wasn't designing for a finished brand; I was designing with one as it took shape.

That meant building a visual system flexible enough to absorb changes in direction without falling apart, while still committing to a clear aesthetic point of view from day one. Every decision had to serve both the brand's evolving identity and the site's functional needs as an e-commerce platform.

Process
01
Brand &
Market Audit
Deep reading of the brand manifesto combined with a competitive audit of niche and luxury fashion leaders — Gucci, Balenciaga, Prada — to understand the visual codes of the segment and identify where the brand could differentiate.
02
UX
Mapping
Mapping the complete user journey — discovery, browsing, product selection, size picking, bag, checkout. Identifying where friction typically occurs in fashion e-commerce and how to reduce it.
03
Design
System
Built a foundational system first — typography scale, color palette, spacing, components — to ensure consistency across every screen before designing individual pages.
04
Responsive
Design
Every screen designed twice: desktop and mobile. Not just scaled down — reconsidered for touch, for smaller viewports, for different browsing contexts.
05
Interactive
Prototype
Built a clickable prototype in Figma covering the full purchase flow — homepage through checkout to order confirmation. Used to validate the experience end-to-end and hand off to development with no ambiguity.
Key Decisions

Every design decision was tested against one question: does this serve the brand's philosophy, or does it contradict it?

—
Why minimal navigation rather than a traditional header menu?
The brand rejects noise. A sparse, typographic navigation forces focus onto the product. Every element that isn't essential to the journey is removed.
—
Why editorial-style imagery over standard product shots?
Generic catalog-style product photos would disconnect the clothing from its intended context. Editorial framing communicates how the pieces are meant to be worn and felt.
—
Why a horizontal carousel for related products instead of a grid?
A grid pulls the user's attention outward into many options, which fights against focus. A single-row horizontal carousel presents one curated item at a time with a clear gesture to browse more — it respects the brand's philosophy of intentional selection over abundance.
—
Why monochrome with the palest possible grey background?
The clothes are the color. Introducing competing hues into the interface would fight for attention. The background should be as neutral as possible — like a gallery wall.
Screens Delivered
Scope

6 desktop screens + 5 mobile screens, plus a complete design system with reusable components. Full end-to-end coverage of the purchase journey.

Desktop — Full Journey
Mobile — Fully Responsive
Design System

* View the full interactive Figma prototype →

Reflection
What I Learned

Designing for a brand with a strong point of view is different from designing for a generic product category. The brand's philosophy becomes a design constraint — and constraints make better decisions.

What worked
Design system first
Building the component library and establishing typographic scale before designing any pages paid off immediately. Consistency across 11 screens came naturally because the system was already in place. No backtracking, no rework.
What worked
Treating brand voice as a constraint
Every ambiguous design decision became easy once I started asking "does this serve raw honesty?" The brand manifesto wasn't just inspiration — it was a decision-making tool. I'd bring this approach to every project going forward.
What I'd do differently
Earlier user research
I worked primarily from the brand brief and my own assumptions about fashion e-commerce users. With more time, I'd have conducted even a small round of informal interviews to validate that the browsing flow matched how actual shoppers think about discovering and evaluating niche clothing.
What I'd do differently
Accessibility audit
The minimalist palette and light type weights look refined, but a rigorous WCAG contrast audit wasn't part of my scope on this project. In a next iteration, I'd stress-test the design against contrast ratios, font legibility at small sizes, and keyboard navigation — making sure the aesthetic doesn't come at the cost of inclusion.
